title: SAML authentication troubleshooting
body:
Use this information to troubleshoot common problems with SAML authentication.

Authentication to realm saml1 failed - Unable to authenticate user

When SAML authentication fails, the error Authentication to realm saml1 failed is logged by the Identity Provider (IdP) or Elasticsearch. This indicates that the SAML response received from the IdP could not be used to authenticate the user against the configured realm.

Common causes include:

1. The certificate used to sign SAML assertions has expired or the signature cannot be verified. Check that xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.saml1.idp.metadata.path points to an up-to-date metadata file.

2. The SAML assertion attributes do not map to any Elasticsearch role. Verify that your role mapping rules in xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.saml1 correctly reference the attributes sent by your IdP.

3. The SP entity ID configured in Kibana does not match what the IdP expects. The entity ID is set via xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.saml1.sp.entity_id and must match the service provider registration in your IdP.

4. Clock skew between the Elasticsearch node and the IdP causes assertion validity checks to fail. SAML assertions include NotBefore and NotOnOrAfter conditions. If the clocks differ by more than a few minutes, assertions will be rejected as expired or not yet valid. The xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.saml1.allowed_clock_skew setting allows tolerance.

5. The NameID format in the SAML response does not match the configured xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.saml1.nameid_format setting.

Authentication failure: Realm saml1 cannot authenticate credentials of type [SamlToken]

This error indicates the SAML token could not be validated at all. Check that:
- The realm is enabled in elasticsearch.yml
- The idp.metadata.path file is readable and contains valid XML
- The sp.acs (Assertion Consumer Service URL) matches the URL Kibana is accessible at

unable to authenticate user [null] for REST request

This occurs when a SAML response arrives but the user principal attribute cannot be extracted. Configure xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.saml1.attributes.principal to point to the correct SAML attribute that carries the username (e.g., NameID or a custom attribute like http://schemas.xmlsoap.org/ws/2005/05/identity/claims/upn).

Credential error: SAML signature validation failed

The signature on the SAML response or assertion failed verification. Possible causes:
- The signing certificate in the IdP metadata is outdated. Refresh the metadata file.
- The assertion is signed with SHA-1 but xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.saml1.idp.metadata.path requires SHA-256.
- The IdP is signing with a certificate not listed in the metadata.

Enable debug-level logging to see the full certificate chain used:
  logger.org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.authc: DEBUG

Authentication realm configuration errors

If xpack.security.authc.realms.saml is misconfigured, Elasticsearch will refuse to start or will log realm initialization failures at startup. The most common configuration errors are:
- Missing or invalid sp.acs URL
- Missing sp.entity_id
- idp.metadata.path file not found
- idp.entity_id not matching the EntityID in the metadata

To validate your realm configuration without restarting, call the Authenticate API after SAML SSO and check the realm field in the response. If you see authentication failure in the Kibana logs with realm configuration context, verify each xpack.security.authc.realms.saml.* setting against the IdP metadata.

Role mapping issues

If authentication succeeds but the user has no roles, the issue is likely in role mapping. SAML attributes are available as metadata in role mapping rules. Use the Roles API to verify that roles exist, then check role_mapping.yml or the Role Mappings API to ensure rules reference the correct SAML attribute names and values. Enable debug logging for org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.authc.support.mapper to trace mapping evaluation.

title: Troubleshoot authorization errors and role mapping
body:
Use this information to troubleshoot common problems with authorization and role mapping in Elasticsearch.

Authorization exception: action [indices:data/read/search] is unauthorized for user

This error means the authenticated user does not have the required privilege. Authentication succeeded — the user's identity was confirmed — but their assigned roles do not grant the action. Check:
1. Which roles are assigned to the user via GET /_security/user/<username>
2. Whether those roles exist and have the correct index privileges via GET /_security/role/<rolename>
3. Whether a role mapping rule is preventing correct role assignment

LDAP group membership not reflected in roles

When using LDAP realm authentication (xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap), Elasticsearch maps LDAP groups to roles using role mapping. If the expected roles are not being applied:
- Verify LDAP groups are being retrieved. Enable debug logging: logger.org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.authc: DEBUG. Look for group DNs in the log output.
- Check that role_mapping.yml contains rules matching those exact group DNs, or that the Role Mappings API has the corresponding entries.
- The realm setting xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap.ldap1.user_group_attribute controls which LDAP attribute is used for group membership.

Credential error vs. authorization error

Distinguish between credential errors (authentication failure — wrong password, realm misconfiguration, expired token) and authorization errors (user authenticated but lacks privilege). The HTTP status codes differ: 401 for authentication failure, 403 for authorization failure. Check the Elasticsearch log for the specific exception type.

Role mapping not applying to realm users

If role mappings exist but users from a specific realm (LDAP, SAML, PKI) are not receiving mapped roles:
- Verify the realm field in role mapping rules matches the realm name exactly (e.g., realm.name: ldap1)
- Verify that the realm is in the correct order in xpack.security.authc.realms
- If using a file-based role mapping (role_mapping.yml), the file is read on the data path and must exist on all nodes

Debugging the mapping pipeline

Enable debug logging for the role mapper:
  logger.org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.authc.support.mapper: DEBUG

This logs each rule evaluation against the user's metadata, including which rules matched and which roles were granted. This is the most effective way to diagnose why expected roles are not assigned after a credential verification succeeds.

Native realm users with missing roles

For native realm users, role assignment is stored internally. If a user's roles were removed, use PUT /_security/user/<username> to reassign them. Changes take effect on the next authentication — there is no need to restart Elasticsearch.

title: Diagnose password setup and connection failures
body:
Use the following information to troubleshoot common problems with password setup and connection issues when using Elasticsearch security features.

Error: PKIX path building failed

This SSL/TLS error occurs when the certificate chain for an Elasticsearch HTTPS connection cannot be validated. The full error message typically reads:
  sun.security.validator.ValidatorException: PKIX path building failed: sun.security.provider.certpath.SunCertPathBuilderException: unable to find valid certification path to requested target

This means the client (curl, Kibana, or another service) does not trust the certificate presented by Elasticsearch. Solutions:
- If using a self-signed certificate, add the CA certificate to the client's truststore or pass it via --cacert in curl.
- Verify that xpack.security.http.ssl.certificate_authorities points to the correct CA file in elasticsearch.yml.
- For Kibana, set elasticsearch.ssl.certificateAuthorities in kibana.yml.

Error: Hostname verification failed

  javax.net.ssl.SSLPeerUnverifiedException: Certificate for <host> doesn't match any of the subject alternative names: [...]

This occurs when the server's certificate does not include the hostname being used to connect. The Subject Alternative Names (SANs) in the certificate must include the hostname or IP address. Regenerate the certificate using elasticsearch-certutil with the correct --dns or --ip flags, or set xpack.security.http.ssl.verification_mode: certificate (which skips hostname verification but is not recommended for production).

Connection refused on port 9200 after enabling security

After enabling xpack.security.enabled: true and TLS, if port 9200 returns connection refused:
- Verify that xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true is set and the certificate and key files exist and are readable by the elasticsearch user.
- Check the Elasticsearch logs for SSL handshake errors at startup.
- Use openssl s_client -connect localhost:9200 to test the TLS handshake directly.

Password setup fails with 'Unable to authenticate user [elastic]'

If the elasticsearch-setup-passwords tool reports this error, the elastic user's password may have already been set. Use the reset password API:
  POST /_security/user/elastic/_password
Or use the elasticsearch-reset-password CLI tool.

title: Troubleshoot Active Directory account lockout
body:
Use the following information to troubleshoot Active Directory realm issues in Elasticsearch, particularly around account lockout caused by failed login attempts.

Active Directory account lockout due to Elasticsearch realm ordering

If your organization's Active Directory accounts are getting locked out, the cause is often the order of realm configuration in xpack.security.authc.realms. When a user authenticates, Elasticsearch tries each realm in order. If a native realm is configured before the Active Directory realm, the native realm may attempt to bind to Active Directory using the submitted credentials even when the user does not exist in the native realm.

To resolve this, ensure that the Active Directory realm is listed before the native realm in xpack.security.authc.realms, or use separate credential paths.

Failed login attempts triggering Active Directory lockout policy

Active Directory enforces lockout policies after a configurable number of failed login attempts. When Elasticsearch retries credentials against multiple realms, each failed attempt counts toward the AD lockout threshold. Symptoms:
- Users report being locked out after what they believe was a single failed login attempt.
- Active Directory event logs show multiple authentication failures from the Elasticsearch node IP.

The xpack.security.authc.realms.active_directory.ad1 realm uses the user's credentials to bind to Active Directory. If credentials are incorrect, the bind fails and is logged as a failed login event in AD.

Diagnosing realm ordering issues

Enable debug logging for the authentication subsystem:
  logger.org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.authc: DEBUG

This shows the order in which realms are consulted and the outcome of each attempt. If the native realm is being tried first for AD users, reorder the realms.

Preventing unnecessary failed authentication attempts

Set order values explicitly in xpack.security.authc.realms to control which realm handles which user population. Consider using the domain_name or url settings in the AD realm to restrict which users it handles, reducing spurious authentication attempts against AD for users that should be served by other realms.

title: LDAP user authentication
body:
You can configure Elasticsearch to communicate with a Lightweight Directory Access Protocol (LDAP) server to authenticate users. LDAP authentication is part of the Elastic Stack security features.

To integrate with LDAP, you configure an LDAP realm in the xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap namespace in elasticsearch.yml. The realm has two modes of operation: user search and user DN templates.

User search mode

In user search mode, Elasticsearch first binds to the LDAP server using a bind DN and password, then searches for the user by their username. Configure:
  xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap.ldap1:
    order: 0
    url: 'ldaps://ldap.example.com:636'
    bind_dn: 'cn=ldapuser,dc=example,dc=com'
    user_search:
      base_dn: 'dc=example,dc=com'
      filter: '(cn={0})'

User DN templates mode

In DN template mode, the user's DN is constructed directly from the username without a search. This is faster but less flexible:
  xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap.ldap1:
    order: 0
    url: 'ldaps://ldap.example.com:636'
    user_dn_templates:
      - 'cn={0},ou=users,dc=example,dc=com'

Group-based role mapping

After authentication, Elasticsearch maps LDAP group memberships to roles. Groups are retrieved from the member_of attribute on the user entry, or via a group search. The xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap.ldap1.user_group_attribute setting specifies which attribute to use.

Role mappings can be defined in role_mapping.yml or via the Role Mappings API. A typical role mapping rule maps a specific LDAP group DN to an Elasticsearch role:
  my_role:
    - realm: { name: 'ldap1' }
      groups: [ 'cn=admins,dc=example,dc=com' ]

SSL/TLS configuration

When connecting to LDAP over TLS (ldaps://), configure the CA certificate:
  xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap.ldap1:
    ssl:
      certificate_authorities: '/etc/elasticsearch/certs/ldap-ca.pem'

Troubleshooting authentication failures

If LDAP authentication fails, check:
1. The bind DN and password are correct — a bind failure prevents user search.
2. The user_search.base_dn is broad enough to include all users.
3. SSL certificate verification is passing (check for PKIX errors in the logs).
4. Group membership attributes are populated correctly in the directory.

Enable debug logging to trace the full credential verification and group retrieval process:
  logger.org.elasticsearch.xpack.security.authc: DEBUG

title: Elasticsearch ML model deployment
body:
Elasticsearch can deploy trained NLP models on ML nodes for local inference. Deployed models are used by inference endpoints, ingest pipeline inference processors, and semantic_text fields.

Supported model types

- BERT-based dense embedding models (for dense_vector and semantic_text fields)
- ELSER (Elastic Learned Sparse EncodeR) — sparse vector model optimized for semantic search without the need for large ML nodes
- Cross-encoder rerankers
- Named entity recognition (NER) models
- Text classification models
- Zero-shot classification models

Deploying a model

Download and deploy a model from the Eland CLI:
  eland_model_hub_import --cloud-id <cloud-id> --es-api-key <key> --hub-model-id sentence-transformers/msmarco-MiniLM-L-12-v3 --task-type text_embedding

Or deploy a pre-packaged model from the ML trained models UI in Kibana.

Allocation settings

When starting a model deployment, configure:
- num_allocations: Number of model allocations across ML nodes. More allocations = higher throughput.
- num_threads: Threads per allocation. Higher threads = lower latency for single requests.

Autoscaling

For Elastic Cloud Hosted, ML model autoscaling automatically adjusts the number of allocations based on request throughput. Set adaptive_resources: { enabled: true } to enable.

ELSER recommendations

ELSER produces sparse vectors and does not require dense_vector fields. It is optimized for English text and performs well on out-of-domain data without fine-tuning. ELSER is recommended when:
- You have English text
- You cannot fine-tune a dense embedding model for your domain
- You want semantic search without managing vector dimensions

Multilingual E5 small

The multilingual-e5-small model supports 100+ languages and is available as a pre-packaged model. It produces 384-dimensional dense vectors. A good default for multilingual semantic search.

title: Elasticsearch index templates
Elasticsearch transforms
Elasticsearch Watcher alerting
Elasticsearch ES|QL query language
body:
ES|QL (Elasticsearch Query Language) is a pipe-based query language for filtering, transforming, and computing analytics over Elasticsearch data.

Basic syntax

ES|QL queries consist of a source command followed by zero or more processing commands, separated by pipe (|) characters:
  FROM my-index
  | WHERE status == "error"
  | STATS count = COUNT(*) BY host
  | SORT count DESC
  | LIMIT 10

Source commands

FROM: Read from one or more indices.
  FROM logs-*, metrics-nginx-*

ROW: Generate rows with literal values (useful for testing).
  ROW a = 1, b = "hello"

SHOW: Show metadata about Elasticsearch (e.g., SHOW INFO).

Processing commands

WHERE: Filter rows.
  | WHERE response_code >= 400

STATS: Compute aggregations.
  | STATS avg_latency = AVG(latency), total = COUNT(*) BY service

SORT: Sort results.
  | SORT @timestamp DESC

LIMIT: Limit number of rows returned.
  | LIMIT 1000

EVAL: Compute new columns.
  | EVAL error_rate = error_count / total_count

KEEP / DROP: Select or remove columns.
  | KEEP @timestamp, host, message

RENAME: Rename columns.
  | RENAME host AS hostname

DISSECT / GROK: Extract structured fields from strings.
  | DISSECT message "%{method} %{path} HTTP/%{version}"

Semantic search in ES|QL

The match (:) operator triggers semantic search on semantic_text fields:
  FROM docs METADATA _score
  | WHERE body: "how to fix cluster health"
  | SORT _score DESC
  | LIMIT 10

Partial results in 9.2+

ES|QL returns partial results by default when some shards are unavailable. Set allow_partial_search_results: false to fail on partial results.

title: Elasticsearch reindexing
Elasticsearch Kibana Discover and dashboards
body:
Kibana Discover is the data exploration interface for Elasticsearch. It allows you to search, filter, and visualize document data interactively.

Searching in Discover

Kibana Query Language (KQL) is the default search syntax in Discover:
  response_code: 500 and url: /api/*
  level: "error" and @timestamp > "2026-06-01"

Lucene query syntax is also supported (switch in the search bar settings).

Filtering

Add filters by clicking field values in the Documents pane or using the Add filter control. Filters are applied as Elasticsearch filter clauses (not scored, cached).

Data views

A data view (formerly index pattern) defines which indices Discover searches and maps field types. Create or manage data views in Stack Management > Data Views.

Saved searches

Save a search configuration (query, filters, columns, sort) for reuse in dashboards.

Dashboards

Kibana Dashboards combine visualizations (Lens panels, Vega, Maps, etc.) into interactive displays. Panels share time range and filter context.

Lens visualizations

Lens is Kibana's drag-and-drop visualization editor. It supports bar charts, line charts, pie charts, metric panels, treemaps, heatmaps, and more. Lens generates ES|QL or aggregation queries automatically from visual configuration.

Kibana alerting

Kibana Rules define alert conditions and trigger actions (email, Slack, webhook, PagerDuty). Rules are evaluated on a configurable schedule and can query Elasticsearch data, APM, security events, and more.

title: Elasticsearch vector storage optimization
Elasticsearch enrich policies
body:
Enrich policies add data from existing indices to incoming documents during ingest. Use the enrich processor in an ingest pipeline to look up and append related data.

How enrich policies work

1. Create an enrich policy that specifies the source index, match field, and enrich fields.
2. Execute the policy to create an enrich index (a read-only index optimized for lookups).
3. Use the enrich processor in an ingest pipeline to look up the match field value and append enrich fields to the incoming document.

Creating an enrich policy

  PUT /_enrich/policy/user-lookup
  {
    "match": {
      "indices": "users",
      "match_field": "user_id",
      "enrich_fields": ["name", "email", "department"]
    }
  }

Execute the policy to build the enrich index:
  POST /_enrich/policy/user-lookup/_execute

Using the enrich processor

  PUT /_ingest/pipeline/enrich-user
  {
    "processors": [
      {
        "enrich": {
          "policy_name": "user-lookup",
          "field": "user_id",
          "target_field": "user",
          "max_matches": 1
        }
      }
    ]
  }

The enrich processor looks up the value of the field in the enrich index and copies matching enrich fields to target_field.

Enrich policy types

match: Exact match (most common).
geo_match: Geospatial match — enriches documents based on whether their coordinates fall within a geo_shape in the source index.
range: Range match — enriches based on whether a value falls within a range in the source index.

Maintaining enrich indices

Enrich indices are snapshots of the source data at execution time. Re-execute the policy to refresh the enrich index when source data changes. The old enrich index remains active until the new one is fully built.

title: Elasticsearch cluster management best practices
body:
This page covers best practices for running Elasticsearch in production.

Sizing guidelines

Heap: Set to 50% of available RAM, capped at 26 GB. Keep the other 50% for the OS page cache and Lucene off-heap structures.

Shard sizing: Aim for shards of 10-50 GB. Shards that are too small waste overhead; shards that are too large slow down recovery.

Shard count per node: No more than 20 shards per GB of heap. Exceeding this increases memory pressure and GC pauses.

High availability

For production clusters:
- At least 3 master-eligible nodes to form a quorum and tolerate single-node failure.
- Replicas for all primary shards (at least 1 replica per primary).
- Dedicated master nodes on large clusters (nodes with only the master role).
- Cross-zone or cross-rack allocation awareness to prevent correlated failures.

Disk management

Monitor disk watermarks:
- Low watermark (85%): No new shards allocated to this node.
- High watermark (90%): Existing shards relocated away from this node.
- Flood stage (95%): Affected indices become read-only.

Preventive actions:
- Delete old indices or move to cold/frozen tier before hitting watermarks.
- Use ILM to automate lifecycle and prevent disk saturation.

Network

Dedicate a network interface to Elasticsearch. Low-latency, high-bandwidth networking between nodes reduces recovery time and improves cluster stability.

Security checklist

- Enable TLS on transport and HTTP layers.
- Use API keys or native realm for application authentication.
- Enable audit logging for security events.
- Apply principle of least privilege for all roles.
- Rotate API keys and credentials periodically.

title: Elasticsearch 9.x what's new overview
body:
Elasticsearch 9.0 is a major version release that introduces breaking changes, removes deprecated features, and adds new capabilities. Key changes in the 9.x series:

Elasticsearch 9.0 highlights

Vector search improvements:
- BBQ (Binary Bit Quantization) for dense_vector fields, providing 32x memory reduction.
- exclude_source_vectors enabled by default in 9.2, reducing storage for vector-heavy indices.
- _knn_search API removed — use the knn retriever or knn option in _search instead.

Security hardening:
- LDAP bind DN without password now prevents node startup.
- TLSv1.1 removed — minimum TLS version is now 1.2.
- Frozen indices removed — use the frozen data tier with partially-mounted searchable snapshots.

HTTP status codes:
- Circuit breaker rejections and backpressure now return HTTP 429 instead of 5xx.

Elasticsearch 9.1 highlights

Retriever improvements:
- Linear retriever with multi-field query format (automatically routes to semantic or lexical sub-retrievers by field type).
- L2 normalization for linear retriever.
- AWS SDK v2 migration for discovery-ec2 and repository-s3 plugins.

Elasticsearch 9.2 highlights

ES|QL partial results:
- ES|QL now returns partial results by default when some shards are unavailable.
- Streams page in Kibana for centralized data stream management.
- Linear retriever top-level normalizer (applies as default to all sub-retrievers).

Elasticsearch 9.4 highlights

ILM and TSDB:
- ILM downsampling force merge disabled by default.
- TSDB (Time Series Data Streams) disables sequence numbers by default for improved write performance.

Upgrade notes

Review breaking changes before upgrading from 8.x to 9.x. Key migration tasks include:
- Migrating frozen indices to the frozen data tier.
- Updating kNN search to use the retriever framework.
- Reviewing TLS configuration to remove TLSv1.1.
- Updating monitoring logic that relied on 5xx responses from circuit breakers.

title: Elasticsearch 8.18 release notes
body:
Elasticsearch 8.18 is the final minor release in the 8.x series, serving as the recommended upgrade path before migrating to 9.0. Key features and changes:

Elasticsearch 8.18 highlights

Upgrade readiness:
- The Upgrade Assistant in Kibana 8.19 and 8.18 identifies deprecated settings and APIs that must be addressed before upgrading to 9.0.
- Machine learning indices created before 8.x must be reindexed before upgrading to 9.x. Initiate this from the Upgrade Assistant in Kibana 8.18 or 8.19.

Security:
- xpack.security.authc.realms configuration is validated more strictly. Realms with duplicate names or invalid configurations generate startup errors in 8.18 in preparation for 9.0 enforcement.
- PKI realm delegation improvements for clustered deployments.

Vector search in 8.18:
- int8 and int4 quantization for dense_vector fields available (introduced in 8.15).
- BBQ quantization preview available in 8.18 as a technical preview.
- semantic_text field type stable (first available in 8.11, stable in 8.15+).

Retriever framework in 8.18:
- rrf retriever stable.
- linear retriever in technical preview (promoted to GA in 9.1).

Breaking changes in 8.18 vs. 9.0:
- In 8.18, frozen indices still work but are deprecated. Migration to the frozen tier is required before upgrading to 9.0.
- TLSv1.1 is deprecated in 8.18 and will be removed in 9.0. Configure your deployments for TLSv1.2 minimum before upgrading.
- cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.enable_for_single_data_node is deprecated in 8.18 and removed in 9.0.

title: Elasticsearch 8.15 release notes
body:
Elasticsearch 8.15 introduced key features for semantic search and vector storage that set the foundation for the 9.x architecture.

Elasticsearch 8.15 highlights

semantic_text field type (stable):
- The semantic_text field type became stable in 8.15 after introduction as a technical preview in 8.11. It provides automatic embedding generation at ingest time, chunking, and seamless semantic match queries without manual inference pipeline setup.
- Supported inference endpoint types: Elasticsearch service (ELSER, multilingual-e5), EIS, Cohere, OpenAI, Hugging Face.

Vector quantization:
- int8 scalar quantization for dense_vector fields becomes GA in 8.15.
- int4 quantization introduced as technical preview.
- BBQ (Binary Bit Quantization) not yet available (technical preview in 8.18, GA in 9.0).

Retriever framework:
- rrf retriever stable.
- text_similarity_reranker retriever (cross-encoder reranking) introduced in 8.15.

ELSER v2:
- ELSER v2 (.elser-2-elasticsearch) is the default sparse embedding model. Improved multilingual and out-of-domain performance over v1.

xpack.security changes in 8.15:
- xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap configuration validation improvements.
- JWT authentication realm enhancements for role mapping.

ILM changes in 8.15:
- ILM downsampling action improvements.
- Configurable force merge in ILM downsampling (force merge enabled by default in 8.15; disabled by default in 9.4).

Note: 8.15 is the minimum version recommended for semantic_text production use. Clusters upgrading from earlier versions to 9.x should pass through 8.15 or later to enable semantic_text indices before the upgrade.

title: Elasticsearch relevance tuning and explain API
Elasticsearch search after and pagination
body:
Elasticsearch provides multiple mechanisms for paginating through search results.

From and size

The simplest pagination uses from (offset) and size (page size):
  { "from": 10, "size": 10 }

Limitation: max_result_window (default 10,000) limits the sum of from + size. This prevents deep pagination from consuming excessive memory. Do not increase max_result_window beyond 50,000 without careful consideration.

Deep pagination alternatives

search_after: Cursor-based pagination that avoids heap overhead for deep pages. Requires a sort and uses the sort values of the last returned document as the cursor:
  {
    "sort": [{ "@timestamp": "desc" }, { "_id": "asc" }],
    "search_after": ["2026-06-01T00:00:00Z", "doc-001"]
  }

Each page uses the sort values from the last document as the next cursor. No limit on depth.

Point in Time (PIT)

For consistent pagination, create a PIT to freeze the index state:
  POST /my-index/_pit?keep_alive=1m

Use the pit_id in subsequent searches to get consistent results even as the index is updated:
  {
    "pit": { "id": "<pit-id>", "keep_alive": "1m" },
    "sort": [{ "@timestamp": "desc" }]
  }

PIT + search_after is the recommended approach for deep, consistent pagination.

Scroll API (deprecated)

The scroll API was the previous approach for deep pagination. It is now deprecated in favor of PIT + search_after. The scroll API is not supported for cross-cluster search.

Pagination and relevance scores

For ranked results, use from/size or PIT + search_after with sort: ["_score", ...]. For offset-based access without relevance ranking (e.g., listing all documents), search_after with a consistent sort field is most efficient.

title: Elasticsearch breaking changes
body:
This page documents breaking changes introduced in each Elasticsearch release. Review these before upgrading.

Elasticsearch 9.0.0 breaking changes

Authentication
- LDAP bind DN without password now prevents node startup. Previously, configuring a bind_dn in xpack.security.authc.realms.ldap without a corresponding bind_password was silently accepted. In 9.0.0, Elasticsearch will refuse to start if a bind DN is configured without a password.

Transport Layer Security
- TLSv1.1 support removed. The minimum supported TLS version is now TLSv1.2. Deployments still using TLSv1.1 must upgrade client libraries before upgrading to 9.0.

Frozen indices
- Frozen indices are removed in 9.0.0. The _freeze and _unfreeze APIs are removed. Frozen index functionality has been superseded by the frozen tier with partially-mounted searchable snapshots. Migrate frozen indices to the frozen data tier before upgrading.

kNN search API removed
- The _knn_search API endpoint is removed in 9.0.0. Use the knn option in the standard _search API instead. The knn retriever (via the retrievers framework) is the recommended approach for vector search.

Routing settings
- cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.enable_for_single_data_node is removed in 9.0.0. The behavior it controlled (enabling disk watermark checks on single-node clusters) is now always enabled.

HTTP status codes
- Requests that exceed the circuit breaker or are rejected due to backpressure now return HTTP 429 (Too Many Requests) instead of 5xx status codes. Update any monitoring or retry logic that keys on 5xx to also handle 429.

Elasticsearch 9.1.0 breaking changes

AWS SDK migration
- The discovery-ec2 and repository-s3 plugins have been migrated from AWS SDK v1 to AWS SDK v2. Configuration property names and credential chain behavior may differ. Review your EC2 discovery and S3 snapshot repository configurations.

Elasticsearch 9.2.0 breaking changes

ES|QL partial results
- ES|QL now returns partial results by default when some shards are unavailable. Previously, ES|QL queries would fail entirely if any shard was unavailable. Set allow_partial_search_results: false to restore the previous behavior.

Vector source exclusion
- exclude_source_vectors is now enabled by default for dense_vector and semantic_text fields. Source vectors are excluded from _source to reduce storage. If your application reads vector values directly from _source, disable this default or use the vectors API.

Elasticsearch 9.4.0 breaking changes

ILM downsampling
- The ILM downsampling action no longer performs a force merge by default. The forced_merge action in ILM downsampling is disabled by default to reduce overhead. Add an explicit force merge step if needed.

TSDB sequence numbers
- Time Series Data Streams (TSDB) now disable sequence numbers by default to improve write performance. If you rely on sequence numbers for optimistic concurrency control in TSDB indices, re-enable them via index settings.

title: JVM settings for Elasticsearch
body:
Elasticsearch runs in a Java Virtual Machine (JVM). The JVM settings control heap size, garbage collection, and diagnostic output. These settings are configured in jvm.options and jvm.options.d/ override files.

Heap size configuration

Set the minimum and maximum JVM heap using Xms (initial heap) and Xmx (maximum heap). Always set Xms and Xmx to the same value to avoid heap resizing pauses.

Recommended sizing rules:
- Set heap to no more than 50% of available RAM, leaving the rest for the OS page cache and Lucene off-heap memory.
- Do not exceed 26-30 GB. Beyond this threshold, the JVM switches from compressed ordinary object pointers (OOPs) to full 64-bit pointers, increasing memory overhead and reducing effective heap. The exact threshold depends on JVM version and platform; 26 GB is a safe upper bound.

Example jvm.options:
  -Xms16g
  -Xmx16g

Out of memory conditions

When the JVM cannot allocate memory, it throws an OutOfMemoryError. This can manifest as:
- A node crashing and restarting
- Circuit breakers tripping (HTTP 429 responses)
- Long GC pauses visible in the Elasticsearch logs as [gc] log lines with duration in milliseconds

Heap dump on out of memory

Configure the JVM to write a heap dump when an OutOfMemoryError occurs:
  -XX:+HeapDumpOnOutOfMemoryError
  -XX:HeapDumpPath=/var/lib/elasticsearch/heapdump.hprof

The heap dump file can be large (up to the heap size). Ensure sufficient disk space at the configured -XX:HeapDumpPath location.

Fatal error logs

For JVM crashes that are not OutOfMemoryError (e.g., SIGSEGV), the JVM writes a fatal error log (hs_err_pid<PID>.log). Set the output path:
  -XX:ErrorFile=/var/log/elasticsearch/hs_err_pid%p.log

Garbage collection logging

GC logs help diagnose memory pressure issues. Enable GC logging in jvm.options:
  -Xlog:gc*,gc+age=trace,safepoint:file=/var/log/elasticsearch/gc.log:utctime,level,pid,tags:filecount=32,filesize=64m

DNS cache settings

Elasticsearch disables the JVM's default positive DNS cache TTL to support dynamic DNS environments. The security manager settings networkaddress.cache.ttl=-1 and networkaddress.cache.negative.ttl=10 are set by default.

JVM performance tuning

Elasticsearch ships with tuned JVM defaults appropriate for most workloads. Avoid changing JVM settings unless directed by Elastic support or you have specific profiling data. Incorrect JVM tuning is one of the most common causes of cluster instability.

Linux OOM killer and exit code 137

When the Linux kernel runs out of memory, the OOM killer terminates processes to free memory. Elasticsearch is a common target due to its large heap. When the OOM killer sends SIGKILL (signal 9) to the JVM process, the process exits with exit code 137 (128 + 9).

Signs the OOM killer terminated Elasticsearch:
- The Elasticsearch process disappears without a JVM fatal error log
- /var/log/kern.log or dmesg contains: Out of memory: Kill process <pid> (java)
- The process exit code is 137

To confirm: check dmesg | grep -i "out of memory" or journalctl -k | grep oom.

Preventing OOM kills:
- Set heap no larger than 50% of available RAM (see Heap size configuration above)
- Use cgroups memory limits so the OOM killer targets the ES container rather than other system processes
- Monitor JVM heap usage via the Nodes Stats API: GET _nodes/stats/jvm

title: Cluster-level shard allocation and routing settings
body:
These settings control how and when Elasticsearch allocates and rebalances shards across the cluster.

cluster.routing.allocation.enable

Controls which shards are allowed to be allocated. Accepted values:
- all (default): Allows all shards to be allocated.
- primaries: Only primary shards are allocated.
- new_primaries: Only primary shards for new indices can be allocated.
- none: No shards are allocated.

Typically set to primaries or none during a rolling upgrade or maintenance window, then reset to null (restoring the default) after the node rejoins:
  PUT /_cluster/settings
  { "persistent": { "cluster.routing.allocation.enable": null } }

cluster.routing.rebalance.enable

Controls shard rebalancing. Values:
- all (default): Allows all shards to be rebalanced.
- primaries: Only primary shards are rebalanced.
- replicas: Only replica shards are rebalanced.
- none: No shard rebalancing.

Disk-based shard allocation watermarks

cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.low
  Default: 85%. Elasticsearch will not allocate new shards to nodes using more than this percentage of disk space.

cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.high
  Default: 90%. Elasticsearch will begin relocating existing shards away from nodes exceeding this threshold.

cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.flood_stage
  Default: 95%. Elasticsearch enforces a read-only index block (index.blocks.read_only_allow_delete) on indices on nodes exceeding this threshold to prevent data corruption.

cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.flood_stage.frozen
  Default: 95%. Applies the flood stage to frozen tier nodes.

cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.flood_stage.max_headroom
  For indices using searchable snapshots, the flood stage is defined as a max headroom value instead of a percentage.

Allocation filtering

cluster.routing.allocation.include.*
cluster.routing.allocation.exclude.*
cluster.routing.allocation.require.*

These settings filter shard allocation by node attributes (e.g., node name, IP, rack). Example — exclude a specific node:
  PUT /_cluster/settings
  { "transient": { "cluster.routing.allocation.exclude._name": "node-3" } }

Awareness attributes

cluster.routing.allocation.awareness.attributes
  Configures allocation awareness to spread shards across availability zones or racks. Set the node attribute used for zone awareness (e.g., rack_id or zone).

cluster.routing.allocation.awareness.force.*
  Forces allocation awareness even if it means leaving shards unassigned.

Concurrent recoveries

cluster.routing.allocation.node_concurrent_recoveries
  Default: 2. Number of concurrent shard recoveries per node.

cluster.routing.allocation.node_initial_primaries_recoveries
  Default: 4. Number of concurrent primary shard recoveries during node startup.

Throttle settings

cluster.routing.allocation.cluster_concurrent_rebalance
  Default: 2. Number of concurrent shard rebalance operations across the cluster.

title: Set up security in self-managed Elasticsearch deployments
body:
Starting in Elasticsearch 8.0, security features are enabled by default for new installations. This page explains how Elasticsearch automatically configures security and what to do for manual security configuration.

Automatic security configuration

When you start Elasticsearch for the first time on a new installation (8.0 and later), it automatically:
1. Enables Elasticsearch security features (xpack.security.enabled: true)
2. Generates TLS certificates for the transport layer
3. Enables TLS on the transport layer
4. Generates enrollment tokens for Kibana and additional Elasticsearch nodes
5. Sets a password for the elastic built-in superuser
6. Outputs the generated password and enrollment token to the terminal

Automatic security is applied only to new single-node installations. Multi-node clusters and upgrades from earlier versions require manual configuration.

Manual security configuration

For environments where automatic security configuration is not appropriate (existing clusters, upgrades, custom certificate requirements), follow the manual setup:

1. Transport TLS: Use elasticsearch-certutil cert to generate CA and node certificates, then configure:
   xpack.security.transport.ssl.enabled: true
   xpack.security.transport.ssl.verification_mode: certificate
   xpack.security.transport.ssl.keystore.path: certs/elastic-certificates.p12

2. HTTP TLS: Generate an HTTP certificate:
   xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
   xpack.security.http.ssl.keystore.path: certs/http.p12

3. Set built-in user passwords: Use the elasticsearch-setup-passwords tool or the Change Password API.

Security in development vs. production

For development (single node, localhost only), you can disable security:
  xpack.security.enabled: false
This is strongly discouraged for production. For production, always use TLS and authentication.

Verifying security configuration

After configuring, verify with:
  curl -u elastic:<password> https://localhost:9200/_cluster/health
A successful response confirms authentication and TLS are working.

title: TLS encryption for cluster communications
body:
This page explains how to secure communications and set up TLS certificates in your Elastic Stack deployments.

For Elastic Cloud Hosted and Elastic Cloud Serverless, communication security is fully managed by Elastic with no configuration required, including TLS certificates.

For ECE, ECK, and self-managed deployments, this page provides configuration guidance for the three main communication channels.

Communication channels

| Channel | Description | TLS requirement |
|---|---|---|
| Elasticsearch transport layer | Node-to-node communication within a cluster | Mutual TLS required for multi-node clusters |
| Elasticsearch HTTP layer | External clients and Elasticsearch REST API (port 9200) | TLS optional but recommended |
| Kibana HTTP layer | Browser and REST clients to Kibana (port 5601) | TLS optional but recommended |

Note: The term transport is used in two contexts. Transport Layer Security (TLS) is the network encryption protocol (the modern name for SSL). The Elasticsearch transport layer is the internal node-to-node communication interface on port 9300 using a binary protocol specific to Elasticsearch.

Transport layer security

The transport layer uses mutual TLS for both encryption and authentication between nodes. This prevents unauthorized nodes from joining the cluster. Securing the transport layer is critical — a malicious actor who can establish a transport connection may be able to invoke system-internal APIs.

For self-managed deployments, transport TLS can be automatically configured via auto-setup, or manually configured using elasticsearch-certutil.

HTTP layer security

The HTTP layer serves the REST API. Enabling TLS here encrypts API traffic between clients (including Kibana) and Elasticsearch. For self-managed deployments, HTTP TLS is configured via:
  xpack.security.http.ssl.enabled: true
  xpack.security.http.ssl.keystore.path: certs/http.p12

For Kibana to trust Elasticsearch's HTTP certificate, set in kibana.yml:
  elasticsearch.ssl.certificateAuthorities: /path/to/ca.crt

Certificate lifecycle management

Certificates have limited lifetimes. For self-managed deployments, you are responsible for monitoring expiration and renewing certificates. For ECK, the operator automatically rotates certificates it manages. For Elastic Cloud Hosted and Serverless, Elastic manages certificate lifecycle entirely.

Index lifecycle management overview
body:
Index Lifecycle Management (ILM) automates the management of indices over time by moving them through phases based on configurable criteria such as index age, size, or document count.

Phases

An ILM policy defines up to five phases:
- Hot: The index is actively written to and searched. Rollover actions can create new indices when thresholds are met.
- Warm: The index is no longer written to but is still queried. Optimization actions like force merge and shrink can be applied.
- Cold: The index is queried infrequently. Searchable snapshots can reduce local storage.
- Frozen: The index is rarely accessed. Data is stored primarily in a snapshot repository; local storage is minimal.
- Delete: The index is deleted.

Rollover

The hot phase typically includes a rollover action that creates a new index when the current index exceeds a threshold:
  "hot": {
    "actions": {
      "rollover": {
        "max_primary_shard_size": "50GB",
        "max_age": "30d",
        "max_docs": 200000000
      }
    }
  }

The rollover action implicitly triggers if a shard reaches 200 million documents, regardless of size settings, to prevent search performance degradation.

Data streams and ILM

Data streams are the recommended way to use ILM with time-series data. A data stream automatically manages backing indices and rollover using an ILM policy referenced in the index template. The write index receives new documents; ILM manages older backing indices.

Applying ILM to a data stream requires only:
1. An ILM policy
2. An index template that references the policy via index.lifecycle.name

For rolling indices (not data streams), you also need to manually create the initial index and alias.

Monitoring ILM

Check the ILM status of an index:
  GET my-index/_ilm/explain

This shows the current phase, action, step, and any errors. Common issues include indices stuck in the warm phase due to unallocated replicas, or frozen phase errors due to missing snapshot repository configuration.

ILM and cluster health

Certain ILM actions (such as rollover in the hot phase and searchable snapshot mounting in the cold phase) require the index to have green cluster health. If the cluster is yellow or red, these actions will be retried but may be delayed.
