Node roles in Elasticsearch
body:
Each node in an Elasticsearch cluster performs one or more roles. Roles are configured via node.roles in elasticsearch.yml.

Default roles

If node.roles is not set, the node receives all roles: master, data, data_content, data_hot, data_warm, data_cold, data_frozen, ingest, ml, remote_cluster_client, transform.

Required roles for a minimum cluster:
- master
- data_content and data_hot, OR data

Available roles

master: Eligible to be elected cluster master. Manages lightweight cluster-wide operations: creating/deleting indices, tracking nodes, allocating shards.

data: Generic data node. Holds shards and handles CRUD, search, and aggregations. Included in all data tiers.

data_content: Content tier. Stores non-time-series data (product catalogs, article archives) optimized for query performance.

data_hot: Hot tier. Entry point for time-series data. Requires fast storage (SSDs).

data_warm: Warm tier. Stores recent but less frequently queried time-series data.

data_cold: Cold tier. Stores older data with lower storage costs. Supports fully-mounted searchable snapshots.

data_frozen: Frozen tier. Stores rarely accessed data almost entirely in a snapshot repository via partially-mounted indices.

ingest: Runs ingest pipelines before indexing. Useful to dedicate as a separate node for heavy ingest workloads.

remote_cluster_client: Can act as a client to remote clusters for cross-cluster search and cross-cluster replication.

ml: Runs machine learning jobs and handles ML API requests.

transform: Runs transforms and handles transform API requests.

Dedicated master nodes

For large clusters, use dedicated master-eligible nodes with only the master role. This prevents master instability from data or ingest workloads:
  node.roles: [ master ]

Coordinating-only nodes

A node with an empty role list acts as a coordinating-only node — it routes requests and handles search scatter/gather but holds no data and is not master-eligible:
  node.roles: [ ]

Repurposing a node

Changing a node's roles requires care. A node that finds unexpected shard data on disk at startup will refuse to start. Before repurposing a data node, use allocation filters to migrate shards off the node first.

title: Red or yellow cluster health status troubleshooting
body:
A red or yellow cluster health status indicates one or more shards are not assigned to a node.

- Red health status: The cluster has unassigned primary shards. Searches and indexing may fail.
- Yellow health status: All primary shards are assigned but some replica shards are unassigned. Increased data loss risk.

Diagnose cluster status

Check cluster health:
  GET /_cluster/health

A healthy cluster shows status: green and unassigned_shards: 0. Yellow means only replicas are unassigned. Red means one or more primaries are unassigned.

View unassigned shards:
  GET /_cat/shards?v&h=index,shard,prirep,state,unassigned.reason

Unassigned shards have state: UNASSIGNED. The prirep value is p for primaries and r for replicas.

Get the root cause for a specific unassigned shard:
  POST /_cluster/allocation/explain
  { "index": "my-index", "shard": 0, "primary": false }

Fix a red or yellow cluster status

Single node cluster: Elasticsearch cannot assign a replica to the same node as the primary. Set index.number_of_replicas: 0 for single-node clusters.

Recover lost nodes: If a data node left the cluster, wait for it to rejoin. Elasticsearch automatically reallocates shards after recovery. Use cluster reroute API to accelerate allocation.

Fix allocation settings: Misconfigured cluster.routing.allocation.include/exclude/require settings can prevent allocation.

Free up disk space: The low disk watermark (cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.low, default 85%) prevents new shard allocation on nodes above this threshold. The high watermark (90%) triggers shard relocation. The flood stage (95%) makes indices read-only.

Re-enable shard allocation: If cluster.routing.allocation.enable was set to none or primaries during maintenance and not reset, shards remain unassigned:
  PUT /_cluster/settings
  { "persistent": { "cluster.routing.allocation.enable": null } }

Reduce JVM memory pressure: High JVM heap usage triggers circuit breakers that stop allocation.

Recover lost primary shard: If a node containing a primary shard is permanently lost, use allocate_stale_primary or allocate_empty_primary with accept_data_loss: true as a last resort. Note: this will cause data loss for the affected shard.

title: Data streams in Elasticsearch
body:
A data stream is an abstraction layer over multiple backing indices optimized for append-only time series data. It provides a single named resource for indexing and search requests.

When to use data streams

Data streams are well-suited for logs, events, metrics, and other continuously generated data. Use a data stream when:
- Your data contains a @timestamp field.
- You mostly index documents, with rare updates or deletes.
- You index without explicit _id, or use first-write-wins behavior for _id.

Backing indices

A data stream consists of one or more hidden auto-generated backing indices. All indexing goes to the write index (the most recently created backing index). Search requests span all backing indices.

A matching index template is required. The template defines mappings, settings, and the ILM policy. Every document must have a @timestamp field mapped as date or date_nanos.

Rollover

Rollover creates a new backing index that becomes the write index. ILM automatically triggers rollover based on the policy. You can also manually roll over a data stream.

The data stream tracks a generation counter (six-digit zero-padded integer). Backing indices are named: .ds-<data-stream>-<yyyy.MM.dd>-<generation>.

Managing data streams

Starting in Elastic Stack 9.2, the Streams page in Kibana provides a centralized interface for managing data streams, consolidating data management tasks without manual configuration.

Append-only nature

Data streams are designed for append-only workloads. To update or delete existing documents, submit requests directly to the document's backing index. For bulk updates, use update by query or delete by query APIs.

title: Upgrade Elasticsearch
body:
This guide outlines the steps for performing a rolling upgrade of a self-managed Elasticsearch cluster.

Cloud deployments (Elastic Cloud Hosted, ECK, ECE, Serverless) handle upgrades automatically — this guide is for self-managed installations only.

Before starting: plan your upgrade and take the upgrade preparation steps. Upgrading from release candidates is not supported. Downgrading to an earlier version is not supported. Do not start an upgrade unless you can complete the rolling upgrade for every node.

Node upgrade order

Upgrade nodes in this order to avoid errors and ensure compatibility:
1. Data nodes, tier by tier: data_frozen → data_cold → data_warm → data_hot → data_content and other data nodes.
2. All remaining non-master, non-data nodes (ml, ingest, coordinating, transform, remote_cluster_client).
3. Master-eligible nodes (master and voting_only) last.

Upgrading master-eligible nodes before data nodes can cause inter-node incompatibilities because upgraded masters may perform cluster state updates incompatible with older-version data nodes.

Upgrade process per node

1. (Optional) Disable shard allocation to prevent unnecessary shard rebalancing during node restart:
   PUT /_cluster/settings
   { "persistent": { "cluster.routing.allocation.enable": "primaries" } }

2. (Optional) Stop ML jobs using the set upgrade mode API to reduce cluster load.

3. Shut down the node (systemd: sudo systemctl stop elasticsearch.service).

4. Upgrade the node using rpm/deb package or by extracting a new tarball to a new directory.

5. Merge configuration changes: leave cluster.initial_master_nodes unset (node is joining existing cluster). Review jvm.options and log4j2.properties for version-specific changes.

6. Upgrade all plugins with elasticsearch-plugin.

7. Start the upgraded node. Verify it joins the cluster via _cat/nodes.

8. Re-enable shard allocation:
   PUT /_cluster/settings
   { "persistent": { "cluster.routing.allocation.enable": null } }

9. Wait for cluster status: green before proceeding to the next node.

Archived settings

Deprecated settings that no longer exist in the target version are archived after upgrade. Remove archived settings to avoid warnings.

title: Authentication in Kibana
body:
Kibana supports multiple authentication mechanisms. After configuring an authentication method in Elasticsearch, you configure an authentication mechanism to log in to Kibana.

Authentication mechanisms

Basic authentication: Username and password via Kibana's login form. Based on the Native, LDAP, or Active Directory realm in Elasticsearch. Only one basic provider per Kibana instance.

Token authentication: Like basic but uses Elasticsearch token APIs. Requires token support enabled in Elasticsearch. Only one token provider per Kibana instance.

PKI authentication: Users authenticate using X.509 client certificates. The certificates are first accepted at the Kibana TLS layer, then validated by an Elasticsearch PKI realm via the Delegate PKI API. PKI authentication does not work if Kibana is behind a TLS termination proxy.

SAML single sign-on: Users authenticate via an external Identity Provider (IdP). Configure with:
  xpack.security.authc.providers:
    saml.saml1:
      order: 0
      realm: saml1

OpenID Connect (OIDC): Similar to SAML, authenticates via an OIDC Provider. Configure with:
  xpack.security.authc.providers:
    oidc.oidc1:
      order: 0
      realm: oidc1

Kerberos: Single sign-on using SPNEGO. Kibana forwards the service ticket to Elasticsearch.

Anonymous authentication: Allows access without credentials. Kibana uses a service account internally. Useful for embedded dashboards:
  xpack.security.authc.providers:
    anonymous.anonymous1:
      order: 0
      credentials:
        username: "anonymous_service_account"
        password: "..."

HTTP authentication: Supports ApiKey, Bearer, Basic, and custom schemes for machine-to-machine access. Kibana relays the Authorization header to Elasticsearch.

Multiple providers

Multiple providers can be configured simultaneously with order priorities. The Login Selector UI shows all configured providers. Use showInSelector: false to hide a provider from the selector while keeping it active.

SSO session management

For SAML and OIDC, Elasticsearch issues access and refresh tokens that Kibana encrypts and stores in its session. Session lifetime is controlled by xpack.security.session.idleTimeout and xpack.security.session.lifespan. When both tokens expire, Kibana redirects to the IdP for re-authentication.

title: Troubleshoot snapshot and restore in Elasticsearch
body:
Use the topics in this section to troubleshoot issues with Elasticsearch snapshots.

Restore from snapshot

When restoring from a snapshot fails, common causes include:
- The snapshot repository is not accessible from all nodes (network or credential issues).
- The target index already exists. Use rename_pattern and rename_replacement in the restore request to restore to a different index name, or delete the existing index first.
- Insufficient disk space on the target nodes for the restored shards.
- Version incompatibility: snapshots can only be restored to the same major version or the next major version. A snapshot from Elasticsearch 8.x can be restored to Elasticsearch 9.x, but not to 7.x.

Troubleshoot broken repositories

A repository can become broken if the underlying storage (S3, GCS, Azure Blob, filesystem) is corrupted or if write operations failed mid-snapshot. Symptoms include errors when listing or accessing snapshots.

Use GET /_snapshot/_all to list repositories and their status. For a broken repository, use POST /_snapshot/<repo>/_verify to check connectivity and integrity.

Diagnose corrupted repositories

Repository corruption typically manifests as missing or inconsistent snapshot metadata. Use the repository analysis API to detect and diagnose corruption:
  POST /_snapshot/<repo>/_analyze

Diagnose unknown repositories

If Elasticsearch references a repository that no longer exists in the cluster state, re-register it with the same name and settings:
  PUT /_snapshot/<repo>
  { "type": "s3", "settings": { "bucket": "...", "region": "..." } }

Fix repeated snapshot policy failures

If an SLM (Snapshot Lifecycle Management) policy keeps failing, check:
1. The repository is accessible and healthy.
2. The SLM policy configuration is valid (GET /_slm/policy/<policy>).
3. The cluster has sufficient disk space and the snapshot process is not being interrupted by node restarts.
4. Examine the last failure via GET /_slm/policy/<policy> — the last_failure field shows the reason.

title: Vector search with kNN in Elasticsearch
Elasticsearch Inference API overview
body:
The Elasticsearch Inference API provides a unified interface for running machine learning inference tasks, including text embedding, text classification, reranking, and completion.

Inference endpoints

An inference endpoint connects Elasticsearch to a model provider and exposes a named endpoint that can be referenced from mappings, search queries, and ingest pipelines.

Create an inference endpoint:
  PUT /_inference/<task_type>/<inference_id>
  {
    "service": "elasticsearch",
    "service_settings": {
      "model_id": ".multilingual-e5-small",
      "num_allocations": 1,
      "num_threads": 1
    }
  }

Supported task types:
- text_embedding: Generate dense vector embeddings from text.
- sparse_embedding: Generate sparse vector representations (ELSER-style).
- rerank: Score and reorder candidate documents.
- completion: Text generation / chat completion.
- chat_completion: Streaming completion.

Elastic Inference Service (EIS)

The Elastic Inference Service provides hosted inference without requiring ML nodes. EIS is automatically enabled for Elastic Cloud Hosted deployments and Serverless projects. For self-managed clusters, connect to EIS via the connect-self-managed-cluster-to-eis documentation.

Default inference endpoints

Elasticsearch pre-creates several inference endpoints for common tasks:
- .elser-2-elasticsearch: ELSER sparse embedding model on ML nodes.
- .multilingual-e5-small-elasticsearch: Dense embedding model on ML nodes.
- Default EIS endpoint for semantic_text fields (changes across versions — specify inference_id explicitly in production).

Running inference

Use the Inference API to run inference directly:
  POST /_inference/text_embedding/my-embedding-endpoint
  {
    "input": "What is the capital of France?"
  }

Inference in ingest pipelines

Use the inference processor in an ingest pipeline to generate embeddings at index time for dense_vector fields (as an alternative to semantic_text's automatic inference).

title: High JVM memory pressure troubleshooting
body:
High JVM memory pressure in Elasticsearch can cause cluster instability, slow queries, and shard allocation failures. The JVM heap is used for caching, query processing, and segment merging.

Symptoms of high JVM memory pressure

- Long garbage collection pauses logged as [gc] overhead exceeded
- Circuit breaker exceptions with HTTP 429 responses
- Node disconnecting from the cluster and rejoining (caused by prolonged GC pauses)
- Shard allocation failures due to circuit breakers
- Slow search responses

Diagnose JVM memory pressure

Check current heap usage:
  GET /_cat/nodes?v&h=name,heap.percent,heap.current,heap.max

Check for recent GC activity:
  GET /_nodes/stats/jvm

The jvm.mem.heap_used_percent field shows current heap pressure. Values consistently above 75-80% indicate pressure. Values above 90% indicate a critical condition.

Circuit breakers

Elasticsearch uses circuit breakers to prevent OutOfMemoryError by rejecting requests before they exhaust heap:
- indices.breaker.total.limit: Overall limit (default: 95% of heap).
- indices.breaker.request.limit: Per-request limit (default: 60%).
- indices.breaker.fielddata.limit: Field data cache limit (default: 40%).

When a circuit breaker trips, requests return HTTP 429 Too Many Requests with a CircuitBreakingException.

Common causes and fixes

Oversized heap: Counterintuitively, a heap above 26-30 GB can cause more GC pressure due to the JVM switching to uncompressed object pointers. Keep heap at 26 GB or below.

Field data cache: Large field data caches can consume significant heap. Set indices.fielddata.cache.size to limit it.

Aggregation queries: High-cardinality aggregations on text fields consume heap for field data. Use keyword fields for aggregations, not analyzed text fields.

Shard count: Too many shards per node increases heap pressure. Follow the guideline of no more than 20 shards per GB of heap.

Query-level fixes: Add query circuit breaker by setting indices.breaker.request.limit. Use search.max_buckets to limit aggregation bucket counts.

title: Elasticsearch mapping overview
body:
Mapping defines the schema for documents in an Elasticsearch index — what fields exist, what types they are, and how they are indexed and stored.

Dynamic vs. explicit mapping

By default, Elasticsearch uses dynamic mapping to automatically detect and add fields when new documents are indexed. Dynamic mapping infers types from JSON values:
- String values become text with a keyword sub-field.
- Integers become long.
- Floats become float.
- Booleans become boolean.
- Nested objects become object.

For production use cases, define explicit mappings to control field types precisely.

Field types overview

text: Analyzed string for full-text search. Tokenized and lowercased by the analyzer at index time. Supports BM25 relevance scoring. Not usable for sorting or aggregations.

keyword: Unanalyzed string for exact matching, sorting, and aggregations. Used for IDs, status codes, tags.

dense_vector: Fixed-length float vector for kNN search.

semantic_text: Text field that automatically generates embeddings via an inference endpoint.

date: Date and datetime values.

long, integer, short, byte, double, float: Numeric types.

object: JSON object with sub-fields. Cannot be queried across nested objects independently.

nested: Array of objects where each object can be independently queried.

Mapping parameters

index: If false, the field is stored but not indexed. Cannot be searched. Default: true.

store: If true, the field value is stored separately from _source. Useful when you need to retrieve specific fields without loading _source.

norms: If false, field-length normalization is disabled. Saves memory for fields where score normalization is not needed (e.g., keyword fields).

fields: Allows the same field to be indexed in multiple ways:
  "title": {
    "type": "text",
    "fields": {
      "keyword": { "type": "keyword" }
    }
  }

This enables full-text search on title and exact-match/aggregation on title.keyword.

Dynamic mapping templates

Dynamic templates customize how dynamically added fields are mapped based on their name or type pattern.

title: Text field type in Elasticsearch
body:
The text field type is used for full-text search. Content is analyzed (tokenized, lowercased, stemmed, etc.) during indexing, and the same analyzer is applied at query time for BM25 full-text search.

Basic mapping

  {
    "body": {
      "type": "text",
      "analyzer": "english"
    }
  }

Analyzers

The analyzer setting controls tokenization and normalization for both indexing and search. Built-in analyzers include:
- standard: Unicode tokenization, lowercase.
- english: Lowercase, English stopword removal, Porter stemming.
- simple: Split on non-letter characters, lowercase.
- whitespace: Split on whitespace only.
- keyword: No tokenization — treats the entire string as a single token (equivalent to keyword field).

For different indexing vs. search analyzers, use the search_analyzer setting.

BM25 scoring

text fields use BM25 (Best Match 25) for relevance scoring by default. BM25 combines:
- TF (term frequency): How often the term appears in the document.
- IDF (inverse document frequency): How rare the term is across all documents.
- Field length normalization: Shorter fields are boosted for the same term frequency.

BM25 scores are based on exact term overlap between query and document. A query for "user can't log in" scores zero against a document that uses only "authentication failure" and "credential error" — even if they describe the same problem.

Multi-fields

A common pattern is to index a field both as text (for full-text search) and keyword (for exact match, sorting, aggregations):
  {
    "title": {
      "type": "text",
      "fields": {
        "keyword": { "type": "keyword", "ignore_above": 256 }
      }
    }
  }

field_data

By default, text fields do not support sorting or aggregations. To enable them, set fielddata: true. This loads the field into heap memory and should be avoided for large, high-cardinality text fields. Use keyword sub-fields for aggregations instead.

Index options

index_options controls what information is stored in the inverted index:
- docs: Document IDs only.
- freqs: Document IDs and term frequencies (default for text).
- positions: Adds position information for phrase queries.
- offsets: Adds character offsets for highlighting.

title: Elasticsearch query DSL overview
body:
The Elasticsearch Query DSL (Domain Specific Language) is a JSON-based query language for searching and filtering documents.

Query context vs. filter context

Queries in query context contribute to the relevance score (_score). Queries in filter context only include/exclude documents without scoring (faster, cacheable).

Leaf query clauses match a specific field against a value:
- match: Full-text search with BM25 scoring. Analyzes the query string.
- term: Exact match on a keyword, numeric, or date field. No analysis.
- range: Match documents where a field value falls within a range.
- exists: Match documents where a field has a value.

Compound query clauses combine leaf clauses:
- bool: Combines must, should, must_not, and filter clauses.
  - must: All clauses must match. Contributes to score.
  - should: At least one clause must match (or 0 if must is present). Contributes to score.
  - must_not: No clause must match. Does not contribute to score.
  - filter: Must match. Does not contribute to score.

Full-text queries

match: The standard query for text search. Analyzes the query string with the same analyzer as the field:
  { "match": { "body": { "query": "cluster health red" } } }

match_phrase: Requires all query terms to appear in the same order with no gaps:
  { "match_phrase": { "body": "circuit breaker exception" } }

multi_match: Runs a match query across multiple fields:
  { "multi_match": { "query": "elasticsearch security", "fields": ["title", "body"] } }

Term-level queries

term: Exact match. Use on keyword fields:
  { "term": { "product": "elasticsearch" } }

terms: Match any of a list of values:
  { "terms": { "version_tags": ["9.0", "9.1"] } }

Scoring and relevance

Scores are computed per-document using BM25 by default. Use the function_score query to inject custom scoring signals (e.g., recency boost, click-through rate). Use script_score for arbitrary Painless scripts.

Sorting

By default, results are sorted by _score descending. Add explicit sort clauses to sort by field values:
  { "sort": [{ "date": { "order": "desc" } }] }

title: Elasticsearch cross-cluster search
body:
Cross-cluster search (CCS) lets you run search requests against one or more remote Elasticsearch clusters. CCS enables federated search across geographically distributed clusters and separation of indexing and search workloads.

Prerequisites

- The coordinating (local) cluster must have the remote_cluster_client node role on at least one node.
- Remote clusters must be registered in the local cluster's configuration.

Configuring remote clusters

Use the remote clusters API or cluster settings:
  PUT /_cluster/settings
  {
    "persistent": {
      "cluster.remote.my-remote-cluster.seeds": ["remote-node-1:9300"]
    }
  }

Two connection modes:
- Sniff mode (default): Connects to seed nodes, discovers the full cluster topology.
- Proxy mode: Routes all connections through a proxy; used in restricted network environments.

Searching across clusters

Prefix index names with <cluster-alias>:<index>:
  GET /local-index,my-remote-cluster:remote-index/_search
  { "query": { "match": { "message": "error" } } }

Search all indices on a remote cluster:
  GET /my-remote-cluster:*/_search

Partial results with CCS

By default, CCS returns partial results if a remote cluster is unavailable. Set skip_unavailable: false on the remote cluster configuration to make CCS fail if a remote is unavailable.

CCS and security

For CCS to work with security enabled, the local cluster must have API key or TLS certificate trust configured for the remote cluster. Use cross-cluster API keys (distinct from regular API keys) to authenticate CCS requests.

CCS limitations

- semantic_text fields: Cross-cluster replication is not supported for indices containing semantic_text.
- Scroll API: Not supported across clusters. Use point-in-time (PIT) with search_after instead.
- Aggregations: Most aggregations work across clusters. Some pipeline aggregations require all data to be on the local cluster.

title: Elasticsearch aggregations overview
Keyword field type in Elasticsearch
body:
The keyword field type stores string values without analysis for exact matching, filtering, sorting, and aggregations.

When to use keyword vs. text

Use keyword for:
- Identifiers (user IDs, index names, status codes, error codes)
- Structured strings (IP addresses, URLs, log levels)
- Values used in aggregations (product names, categories, tags)
- Values used for sorting

Use text for:
- Free-form prose that should be full-text searched
- Fields where users search with natural language queries

Basic mapping

  {
    "status": {
      "type": "keyword"
    }
  }

Key parameters

ignore_above: Strings longer than this value are not indexed. Default: 2147483647 (no limit). Common value: 256 to prevent indexing of very long strings.

normalizer: Applies token filters (but not tokenizers) to keyword values before indexing. Use for case-insensitive keyword matching:
  {
    "status": {
      "type": "keyword",
      "normalizer": "lowercase"
    }
  }

doc_values: Stores field values in column-oriented format for sorting and aggregations. Enabled by default. Disable to save disk if aggregations are never needed.

Eager global ordinals: For high-cardinality keyword fields used heavily in terms aggregations, set eager_global_ordinals: true to pre-build ordinals at refresh time instead of at query time.

Keyword fields and exact-token traps

Keyword fields enable precision retrieval of exact strings. A query for the exact string "cluster.routing.allocation.disk.watermark.low" will match documents that contain exactly this string in a keyword field — this is the mechanism for exact-token trap queries in workshop Lab 2. BM25 on text fields would also match, but keyword fields guarantee exact match semantics with no false positives from partial token overlap.

title: Elasticsearch REST API conventions
body:
The Elasticsearch REST API follows consistent conventions for requests and responses.

HTTP methods

GET: Read operations. Also accepts a request body for complex queries (e.g., GET /_search with a query DSL body).
PUT: Create or update a named resource (index, alias, mapping, settings).
POST: Non-idempotent operations (indexing a document without specifying ID, refresh, search with complex body).
DELETE: Delete a resource.
HEAD: Check existence (returns 200 or 404, no body).

HTTP status codes

200 OK: Successful GET, PUT, DELETE, POST.
201 Created: Document indexed successfully.
400 Bad Request: Malformed request (syntax error, missing required field).
401 Unauthorized: Missing or invalid credentials.
403 Forbidden: Authenticated but insufficient privileges.
404 Not Found: Index, document, or resource does not exist.
405 Method Not Allowed: HTTP verb not supported for this endpoint.
408 Request Timeout: Request timed out.
409 Conflict: Version conflict on optimistic concurrency control.
429 Too Many Requests: Circuit breaker tripped or request rejected due to backpressure. Introduced in 9.0 (previously returned 5xx for some of these cases).
500 Internal Server Error: Unexpected server error.
503 Service Unavailable: Node overloaded or cluster not ready.

Request body

All request bodies must be valid JSON. Responses are also JSON by default. Add ?pretty to format response JSON for human readability.

Multi-index syntax

Many APIs accept comma-separated index lists: GET /index1,index2/_search. Wildcards are supported: GET /logs-*/_search. Use _all or * to target all indices.

Date math in index names

Use date math expressions in index names for time-based index selection:
  GET /<logs-{now/d}>/_search

Common query parameters

pretty: Format JSON response.
format: Response format (json, yaml, smile, cbor).
error_trace: Include stack trace in error responses.
human: Return values in human-readable format (e.g., 5kb instead of 5120).

title: Elasticsearch index settings
body:
Index settings control the behavior of individual Elasticsearch indices. Settings are either static (set at index creation) or dynamic (updateable on a live index).

Static settings (set at creation)

number_of_shards: Number of primary shards. Default: 1. Cannot be changed after creation without reindex. Setting too many shards wastes resources; too few limits throughput.

number_of_routing_shards: Used to split an index. Defines the maximum number of shards the index can be split into.

codec: Compression codec for stored fields. default uses LZ4; best_compression uses DEFLATE (slower writes, smaller storage).

Dynamic settings (updateable)

number_of_replicas: Number of replica shards per primary. Default: 1. Increase for read scalability and fault tolerance. Setting to 0 removes all replicas (yellow cluster status on multi-node).

refresh_interval: How often the index is refreshed to make new documents searchable. Default: 1s. Set to -1 to disable refresh during bulk indexing, then reset after.

max_result_window: Maximum value for from + size in a search. Default: 10000. Increase with caution — large from + size queries are memory-intensive. Use search_after or PIT for deep pagination.

blocks.read_only: If true, index allows only read operations. Set automatically when flood stage watermark is exceeded.

blocks.read_only_allow_delete: Like read_only but allows deletion. Set by the disk flood stage mechanism.

routing.allocation.require/include/exclude: Per-index allocation filters to control which nodes host this index's shards.

Analysis settings

Custom analyzers, tokenizers, token filters, and char filters are defined in the analysis block of index settings. Analysis settings are static — the index must be closed to update them:
  PUT /my-index/_settings
  { "index.analysis.analyzer.my_analyzer.type": "custom" }

Mapping updates

Mappings can be updated to add new fields or change certain parameters. Changing the type of an existing field requires reindex. Use the reindex API to rebuild an index with new mappings.

title: Elasticsearch snapshot and restore overview
body:
Snapshots are backups of a running Elasticsearch cluster. A snapshot captures the state of an index at a point in time and stores it in a snapshot repository.

Snapshot repositories

A repository defines where snapshots are stored. Supported repository types:
- fs: Shared filesystem (all nodes must have access).
- s3: Amazon S3 bucket. Requires repository-s3 plugin.
- gcs: Google Cloud Storage. Requires repository-gcs plugin.
- azure: Azure Blob Storage. Requires repository-azure plugin.
- hdfs: Hadoop Distributed File System.

Register a repository:
  PUT /_snapshot/my-backup-repo
  {
    "type": "s3",
    "settings": {
      "bucket": "my-backup-bucket",
      "region": "us-east-1"
    }
  }

Creating snapshots

Take a snapshot of all indices:
  PUT /_snapshot/my-backup-repo/snapshot-2026-06-19
  { "wait_for_completion": true }

Snapshot a specific index:
  PUT /_snapshot/my-backup-repo/my-snapshot
  { "indices": "my-index" }

Restoring snapshots

  POST /_snapshot/my-backup-repo/my-snapshot/_restore
  {
    "indices": "my-index",
    "rename_pattern": "(.+)",
    "rename_replacement": "restored-$1"
  }

Snapshotted indices must be closed before restore unless you use rename_pattern to restore to a different name.

Searchable snapshots

Searchable snapshots allow indices to be searched directly from a snapshot repository without fully restoring them to local storage. Two mount modes:
- Fully mounted: Index is cached locally on cold tier nodes. Replicas are not needed.
- Partially mounted: Only recently accessed data is cached locally on frozen tier nodes. Much lower local storage requirement.

Snapshot Lifecycle Management (SLM)

SLM automates snapshot creation, retention, and deletion on a schedule:
  PUT /_slm/policy/daily-snapshots
  {
    "schedule": "0 30 1 * * ?",
    "name": "<daily-snap-{now/d}>",
    "repository": "my-backup-repo",
    "retention": { "expire_after": "30d", "min_count": 5 }
  }

title: Elasticsearch security overview
body:
Elasticsearch security features protect your data by controlling access and encrypting communications. Since Elasticsearch 8.0, security is enabled by default on new installations.

Core security features

Authentication: Verifies user identity. Supported authentication realms include native, LDAP, Active Directory, SAML, OIDC, PKI, Kerberos, and JWT. Realms are configured in xpack.security.authc.realms.

Authorization: Controls what authenticated users can do via roles and role mappings. Roles define index and cluster privileges. Role mappings connect realm identities to roles.

Encryption: TLS encrypts the transport layer (node-to-node) and HTTP layer (client-to-node) communications.

Audit logging: Records authentication events, authorization failures, and administrative actions. Enable with xpack.security.audit.enabled: true.

API keys: Machine-to-machine authentication without user credentials. Create with POST /_security/api_key. API keys can have defined privileges, expiry dates, and metadata.

Built-in roles

superuser: Full cluster and index access. Should be used only for administration.
kibana_admin: Full access to Kibana features and spaces.
elastic: Built-in superuser. Password set during installation.

Security realms

Realms are configured in order under xpack.security.authc.realms. Elasticsearch tries each realm in order until one authenticates the user. Each realm has an order setting. The native realm handles users stored in Elasticsearch itself. LDAP and Active Directory realms delegate to external directory services.

Field and document-level security

Field-level security restricts which fields a role can read. Document-level security restricts which documents a role can access via a filter query. These features require at least a Gold subscription.

title: Elasticsearch deployment configuration guide
body:
Elasticsearch is configured via elasticsearch.yml, JVM settings, and environment variables. This page covers the most important configuration settings for self-managed deployments.

Configuration file

The main configuration file is elasticsearch.yml in the config directory. Settings use YAML syntax. Path defaults:
- Config: /etc/elasticsearch/ (rpm/deb) or <install_dir>/config/ (tar)
- Data: /var/lib/elasticsearch/ (rpm/deb)
- Logs: /var/log/elasticsearch/ (rpm/deb)

Cluster name

All nodes in a cluster must share the same cluster.name:
  cluster.name: production-cluster

Node name

  node.name: node-1

Network binding

By default, Elasticsearch binds to localhost. For multi-node clusters:
  network.host: 0.0.0.0
  http.port: 9200
  transport.port: 9300

Discovery

For multi-node clusters, configure seed hosts for discovery:
  discovery.seed_hosts: ["host1", "host2", "host3"]

For initial cluster formation (only set once for bootstrap, then remove):
  cluster.initial_master_nodes: ["node-1", "node-2", "node-3"]

Heap size

Configure via jvm.options:
  -Xms16g
  -Xmx16g

System limits

Elasticsearch requires:
- vm.max_map_count >= 262144 (for mmap)
- ulimit -n >= 65536 (open file descriptors)
- ulimit -u >= 4096 (threads)

Important settings for production

- path.data: Dedicated data directory (SSD recommended).
- path.logs: Log directory.
- bootstrap.memory_lock: true — prevents JVM heap from being swapped.
- action.destructive_requires_name: true — prevents accidental deletion of all indices.

title: Elasticsearch scripting with Painless
Elasticsearch data tiers
body:
Data tiers are a multi-tier storage architecture that lets you balance search performance against storage cost over the lifetime of time-series data.

Tier architecture

Hot tier: Stores the most recently ingested, most frequently queried data. Requires fast hardware (NVMe SSDs). New data stream indices start here. Index lifecycle management rollover targets the hot tier.

Warm tier: Stores data that is queried less frequently. Typically holds data from recent weeks. Hardware can be slower than hot. ILM moves indices from hot to warm after rollover + a configured age.

Cold tier: For rarely queried historical data. Optimized for storage cost over query speed. Supports fully-mounted searchable snapshots (no local replicas needed — data comes from snapshot repository on access).

Frozen tier: For data accessed only occasionally. Uses partially-mounted searchable snapshots with minimal local cache. Query latency is higher due to fetching data from the snapshot repository. Dramatically reduces storage costs for large historical datasets.

Configuring node tiers

Assign nodes to tiers via node.roles:
  node.roles: [ data_hot ]
  node.roles: [ data_warm ]
  node.roles: [ data_cold ]
  node.roles: [ data_frozen ]

For clusters that don't use data tiers, use the generic data role.

ILM phase actions by tier

Hot phase: rollover, forcemerge, shrink, searchable_snapshot.
Warm phase: allocate (move to warm nodes), forcemerge, shrink, readonly.
Cold phase: allocate (move to cold nodes), searchable_snapshot (fully mounted).
Frozen phase: searchable_snapshot (partially mounted).
Delete phase: delete.

Shard allocation to tiers

ILM uses the _tier_preference routing attribute to move indices to the appropriate tier. You can also set this directly:
  PUT /my-index/_settings
  { "index.routing.allocation.include._tier_preference": "data_warm,data_hot" }

title: Elasticsearch circuit breaker settings
body:
Circuit breakers prevent out-of-memory errors by failing requests that would exceed JVM heap limits. They monitor estimated memory usage and return HTTP 429 Too Many Requests when a limit is exceeded.

Total circuit breaker

indices.breaker.total.limit: Overall limit for all circuit breakers combined. Default: 95% of JVM heap. When this is exceeded, all memory-intensive operations are rejected.

Request circuit breaker

indices.breaker.request.limit: Limit for per-request memory (aggregations, sort, etc.). Default: 60% of JVM heap.

indices.breaker.request.overhead: Estimated overhead multiplier for request memory. Default: 1.0.

Field data circuit breaker

indices.breaker.fielddata.limit: Limit for the field data cache (used by sorting and aggregations on text fields). Default: 40% of JVM heap.

indices.breaker.fielddata.overhead: Memory estimation overhead for field data. Default: 1.03.

In flight requests circuit breaker

network.breaker.inflight_requests.limit: Limit for in-flight HTTP requests. Default: 100% of JVM heap (effectively disabled; governed by total breaker).

Accounting circuit breaker

indices.breaker.accounting.limit: Limit for objects held in memory at rest (Lucene segments, etc.). Default: 100% (governed by total).

Script compilation circuit breaker

script.max_compilations_rate: Rate limit for Painless script compilations to prevent excessive compilation overhead. Default: 150/5m (150 compilations per 5 minutes).

Circuit breaker errors

When a circuit breaker trips, Elasticsearch returns HTTP 429 with a CircuitBreakingException in the response body. The exception includes the breaker name, the requested memory, and the limit. This information helps diagnose which workload is causing memory pressure.

Monitoring breakers

Check current breaker state:
  GET /_nodes/stats/breaker

title: Elasticsearch cat APIs overview
body:
The cat (compact and aligned text) APIs return human-readable tabular output for cluster and index information. They are intended for human consumption via terminal or Kibana console — not for application use.

Common cat APIs

_cat/health: Cluster health summary with status (green/yellow/red), node count, and shard counts.
  GET /_cat/health?v

_cat/nodes: Node information including name, IP, heap percentage, CPU, load, and node roles.
  GET /_cat/nodes?v&h=name,heap.percent,cpu,load_1m,node.roles

_cat/indices: Index information including health, status, document count, deleted count, store size, and shard counts.
  GET /_cat/indices?v&s=docs.count:desc

_cat/shards: Shard-level details including which node hosts each shard, state (STARTED/UNASSIGNED/INITIALIZING), and size.
  GET /_cat/shards?v&h=index,shard,prirep,state,node,unassigned.reason

_cat/allocation: Disk space usage per node, showing total disk space, used space, available space, and shard count.
  GET /_cat/allocation?v

_cat/recovery: In-progress and recent shard recovery operations.
  GET /_cat/recovery?v&active_only

_cat/count: Document count across all indices or a specific index.
  GET /_cat/count/my-index?v

_cat/segments: Lucene segment information per shard, including segment size and whether the segment is memory-resident.

Query parameters

v: Verbose mode — includes column headers.
h: Column selection. Specify only the columns you need.
s: Sort by column (append :desc for descending).
bytes: Unit for byte values (b, kb, mb, gb).
format: Output format (text, json, yaml, smile, cbor).

title: Elasticsearch Elastic Inference Service (EIS)
Elasticsearch aliases
